name: Texas
tagline: Largest state budget in Texas history, a new standalone cyber agency, and an AI-governance law now in force — Texas is reshaping how it buys and secures technology.

execSummary.summary: Texas enters the 2026–27 biennium with the largest budget in its history ($337.4B) and three structural shifts every SLG seller should know. First, cybersecurity is being pulled out of DIR into a brand-new standalone agency — the Texas Cyber Command in San Antonio — changing where security dollars and decisions sit. Second, the Texas Responsible AI Governance Act (TRAIGA) took effect January 1, 2026, putting real guardrails on government use of AI and creating an AI Advisory Council and regulatory sandbox. Third, an historic semiconductor and data-center buildout (TSIF, Meta, Stargate, TI) is turning Texas into national AI infrastructure. For Dell, the buying motion still runs through DIR Cooperative Contracts, and TX-RAMP cloud certification remains a hard gate on anything touching state data.
opportunities: AI-infrastructure / 'AI factory' demand tied to the Texas data-center and semiconductor boom (PowerEdge, networking, services).
Stand-up of the Texas Cyber Command in San Antonio — secure compute, infrastructure, and managed services for a greenfield agency.
Agency AI adoption under TRAIGA guardrails — a chance to lead with governed, on-prem and private-cloud AI for sensitive workloads.
Endpoint, client, and lifecycle refresh across a record general-government budget.

stats.label: Texas Cyber Command
stats.note: State GR through 2027; HQ San Antonio

budget.intro: Texas budgets on a two-year (biennial) cycle. The 89th Legislature's SB 1 appropriates $337.4B for 2026–27 — roughly $176B in FY2026 and $160B in FY2027 — the largest in state history. There is no single 'state IT budget' line; technology spend is distributed across agencies and a set of named technology and security initiatives funded this biennium.
notes: Funding figures are biennial (two-year) totals unless noted.
Cybersecurity dollars are mid-migration from DIR to the new Texas Cyber Command — confirm which entity owns a given program before forecasting.

initiatives.name: Texas Cyber Command
initiatives.summary: HB 150 (signed June 2025) created the nation's largest state cyber agency, headquartered in San Antonio with UTSA as a partner and led by retired Navy Vice Adm. Timothy 'T.J.' White. It is absorbing DIR's cybersecurity functions in phases — DIR transferred statewide cybersecurity employees and functions on March 1, 2026, with the transition slated to complete by Dec 31, 2026 and people/process/mission integration finalized by Feb 1, 2027 — and stands up a Cyber Threat Intelligence Center, Incident Response Unit, and Digital Forensic Lab serving state and local government. Active hiring for analysts and unit leadership through mid-2026. A greenfield agency means greenfield infrastructure, secure compute, and services needs.

initiatives.name: TRAIGA — Responsible AI Governance Act
initiatives.summary: HB 149 focuses on government use of AI: prohibits manipulative/discriminatory and social-scoring systems, stands up an AI Advisory Council, and opens a 36-month regulatory sandbox. Agencies now need governed, auditable AI — an opening for private/on-prem AI on sensitive data rather than open public models.

initiatives.name: Semiconductor & Data-Center Buildout
initiatives.summary: The Texas CHIPS Act funds the Texas Semiconductor Innovation Fund (~$948M). Alongside it, hyperscale AI data centers are landing fast (Meta's $1.5B El Paso campus scaling toward 1GW; the multi-site Stargate project; TI's new Sherman 300mm fab). This is the strongest tailwind for Dell AI-infrastructure and ISG demand in the state.

initiatives.name: TX-RAMP Cloud Security Program
initiatives.summary: Under DIR, Texas agencies and public higher-ed may only contract for cloud services that hold TX-RAMP certification (NIST 800-53 Low/Moderate baselines). Certification must be maintained for the life of the contract. Any Dell cloud/as-a-service offering touching state data needs the right TX-RAMP level — a gate to clear early, not at close.

procurement.intro: The Texas Department of Information Resources (DIR) is the central technology procurement authority. Most state, local, and higher-ed technology buys run through DIR Cooperative Contracts, which let eligible entities purchase from pre-competed vendor agreements without running their own RFP. DIR also delivers Shared Technology Services and Communications Technology Services.

vehicles.name: DIR Cooperative Contracts
vehicles.detail: Primary vehicle. Pre-competed; open to state agencies, local gov, and higher-ed. Confirm Dell agreements and resellers are current and correctly scoped.

vehicles.name: DIR Shared Technology Services (STS)
vehicles.detail: Data center, cloud, and managed services delivered through DIR for participating agencies.

vehicles.name: TX-RAMP certification
vehicles.detail: Not a contract, but a prerequisite gate for any cloud/as-a-service offering touching state data.

entities.name: DIR
entities.role: Central IT procurement, policy, and (currently) cyber — leads the statewide technology motion.

entities.name: Health & Human Services (HHSC)
entities.role: Among the largest agency IT spenders; data-heavy modernization.

entities.name: Texas Cyber Command
entities.role: New buyer for security infrastructure and services; San Antonio.

entities.name: University systems (UT, Texas A&M)
entities.role: Large independent research-computing and AI-infrastructure buyers.

procurement.competition: Texas is a competitive, contract-driven market — incumbents hold DIR agreements across compute, networking, and services. Differentiation tends to come from TX-RAMP-ready offerings, AI-infrastructure capability, and services that map to the Cyber Command and data-center buildout rather than price alone.

powerStructure.name: Greg Abbott
powerStructure.title: Governor
powerStructure.note: Made Cyber Command an emergency item; champions the semiconductor/AI agenda.

powerStructure.name: Tony Sauerhoff
powerStructure.title: State CIO & DIR Executive Director
powerStructure.note: Confirmed permanent March 2026 (interim from Jan 2026); former Chief AI & Innovation Officer and state CISO. Owns statewide tech direction as DIR sheds cybersecurity to the Cyber Command (functions transferred March 1, 2026).

powerStructure.name: Timothy 'T.J.' White (VADM, USN ret.)
powerStructure.title: Chief, Texas Cyber Command
powerStructure.note: Abbott appointee (term through Feb 1, 2027); former commander, U.S. Fleet Cyber Command / 10th Fleet. Leads the greenfield agency and its Threat Intelligence, Incident Response, and Forensics units.

powerStructure.name: Tan Parker / Giovanni Capriglione
powerStructure.title: Legislative sponsors
powerStructure.note: Lead authors behind Cyber Command (HB 150) and AI/innovation policy.

powerStructure.name: Legislative Budget Board (LBB)
powerStructure.title: Appropriations & fiscal analysis
powerStructure.note: Sizes program costs and staffing — useful for forecasting cyber/AI spend.

news.title: DIR launches Texas Design System to modernize state websites (HB 5195)
news.impact: New statewide, accessibility-first content library (37 components, standardized templates) mandated by HB 5195 (89R) to standardize agency website/portal modernization. Signals a fresh, DIR-led digital-services and web-modernization workstream across agencies — adjacent pull for services, hosting, and modernization engagements.

news.title: DIR-to-Texas Cyber Command cyber transition confirmed on track
news.impact: DIR transferred statewide cybersecurity employees and functions to the Texas Cyber Command on March 1, 2026; the handover is slated to complete by Dec 31, 2026, with people/process/mission integration finalized by Feb 1, 2027. Confirm which entity owns a given security program before forecasting; TX-RAMP still administered under DIR for now.

news.title: DIR 2026–2030 State Strategic Plan pushes AI, cloud, and procurement expansion
news.impact: New five-year plan prioritizes responsible AI adoption (guidance, training, pilots, sandbox), legacy-system retirement, DevSecOps, and business-driven cloud. DIR's AI training-certification program is live (57 programs certified as of May 2026). Sets the funding/procurement direction agencies will follow.

news.title: Abbott directs PUC/ERCOT to make AI data centers fund their own grid costs
news.impact: Sweeping directive: PUC to act on residential transmission costs by July 31 and require data centers to fully fund grid infrastructure; PUC+ERCOT joint memo due July 17. Six 2027-session priorities (added generation, water reporting, repeal of data-center sales-tax exemptions). Signals tighter siting/cost rules ahead even as the buildout continues.

news.title: Tony Sauerhoff confirmed as permanent State CIO & DIR Executive Director
news.impact: Tech direction stabilizes under an AI-forward leader; DIR continues its reorg as cyber moves to the Cyber Command.

news.title: TRAIGA takes effect
news.impact: Government-use AI guardrails are now live; agencies need governed, auditable AI approaches.

dellActions: Lead the AI-infrastructure conversation: tie Dell ISG / AI Factory offerings to the Texas data-center and semiconductor buildout and the university research-computing buyers.
Engage the Texas Cyber Command early as a greenfield account in San Antonio — secure compute, infrastructure, and managed services for a brand-new agency.
Clear the TX-RAMP gate before pursuing cloud/as-a-service deals — confirm the right certification level for any offering touching state data.
Validate that Dell's DIR Cooperative Contract coverage and resellers are current and correctly scoped for the agencies you're targeting.
Position governed, private/on-prem AI for sensitive workloads as agencies operationalize TRAIGA.
